1: The coalition of Canaanite kings is formulaic — wayĕhî kišĕmōaʿ ('when they heard') introduces a chain of reactions to Israel's advance. The geographic sweep ('hill country, western foothills, and along the entire coast') frames the total political landscape Israel now faces. The verb wayyiqqāhălû ('gathered/assembled') carries the sense of formal military muster, not spontaneous assembly.
2: ləlāḥam ʿim-yĕhôšuaʿ wĕʿim-yiśrāʾēl yāḥdāw — 'to fight with Joshua and with Israel as one.' The adverb yāḥdāw (as one, together) signals the unprecedented solidarity of the Canaanite response; seven distinct peoples now act as a single force. The narrative's tension: will this coalition, unlike the Gibeonites, choose war over treaty?
3: The Gibeonites receive the same report the kings did (wayišmĕʿû) but respond differently. The contrast is deliberate: hearing the same news, one group forms a war coalition, another seeks survival through treaty. ʿāśû gam-hēmmāh — 'they also did' — introduces the alternative response with a particle of ironic echo.
4: wayyishtaddĕlû — they acted, provisioned themselves cunningly. The key term is ʿormāh (Joshua 9:4's cognate ʿārûm), meaning cunning or craftiness. The same root describes the serpent in Genesis 3:1 (ʿārûm); in Proverbs, cunning (ʿormāh) can be a virtue (Proverbs 8:5; Joshua 12:23) — wisdom applied strategically — but here it is deployed deceptively. The Gibeonites are clever; whether their cleverness is condemned depends on what Israel should have done at Joshua 9:14.
5: The staging of deception is precise: worn sandals, patched, dry crumbling bread — each detail is verifiable at a glance. niqqudîm ('crumbly' or 'spotted' bread) is used only here and in 1 Samuel 2:36 in a different sense; the bread's state signals great age. The Gibeonites have thought through every element of a convincing disguise.
6: They come to Joshua at Gilgal — Israel's base camp throughout the initial conquest phase (Joshua 4:19; Joshua 5:10; Joshua 10:6). ʾanašîm mērāḥôq bāʾnû — 'men from a distant country we have come.' Their opening claim is the lie around which the entire chapter turns; the Hebrew word order puts 'from a distance' first, fronted for emphasis.
7: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾîš-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-haḥiwwî — 'the men of Israel said to the Hivite.' The Hivites are specifically named — the narrative is already giving the reader information the Israelite leaders lack. Israel's question — 'perhaps you live among us' — reflects awareness of the ḥērem command: the nearby peoples must be destroyed (Deuteronomy 7:2; Joshua 20:17); treaties with them are forbidden (Deuteronomy 7:2). The question is therefore legally crucial, not merely polite.
8: ʿăbādêkā ʾănaḥnû — 'your servants we are.' The idiom is diplomatic submission language, placing themselves under Joshua's protection. The question 'who are you and where do you come from?' follows diplomatic protocol for treaty negotiation. The Gibeonites' repeated use of 'servant' (ʿebed) throughout the chapter eventually shapes their permanent status at Joshua 9:23.
9: The Gibeonites claim they came 'because of the name of YHWH your God' — a remarkable confession from Canaanites. They cite Sihon and Og (Num 21) but notably stop before mentioning Jericho and Ai — recent victories they had certainly heard about (Joshua 9:3, wayišmĕʿû). This selective knowledge claim is part of the deception; they let Israel believe the Gibeonites are too remote to know the latest news.
10: The Gibeonites claim knowledge only of the trans-Jordan victories (Sihon and Og), implicitly denying knowledge of the Cisjordan campaigns. This is calculated omission — they knew (Joshua 9:3) but pretend ignorance. ʾašer ʿāśāh lĕšĕnê malkê hāʾĕmōrî — 'what he did to the two kings of the Amorites': Sihon and Og were paradigmatic proof of YHWH's power (cited repeatedly in Deut and Ps 135-136), the minimum a distant nation might believably know.
11: The Gibeonites claim their elders and all inhabitants sent them. kol-yōšĕbê ʾarṣênû — 'all the inhabitants of our land' — they present themselves as official envoys, not individuals. The appeal to 'elders' (zĕqēnîm) establishes civic legitimacy, making the treaty a formal treaty with a people, not an ad hoc agreement.
12: The theatrical evidence: zeh laḥmênû ḥam hillāʾnûhû — 'this bread of ours was hot [when] we took it.' They present the physical props of deception, inviting Israel to examine the evidence and draw the wrong conclusion. The detail that the bread was 'our provision' (ṣēdāh) frames this as journey-supply, making the claim of distant origin seem verifiable.
13: Wineskins cracked from the journey, sandals worn out — the deception complete. The irony is that Israel is about to make a covenant based on evidence they handled but did not think to question spiritually (Joshua 9:14). The Gibeonites provide exactly enough physical 'proof' to forestall skepticism.
14: wayyiqĕḥû hāʾănāšîm miṣṣēdām wĕʾet-pî YHWH lōʾ šāʾālû — 'the men took from their provisions and the mouth of YHWH they did not ask.' This is the narrative's hinge. šāʾal ʾet-pî YHWH ('ask the mouth of YHWH') is the idiom for oracular inquiry via the high priest's Urim and Thummim (Numbers 27:21; 1 Samuel 23:2). The phrase lōʾ šāʾālû ('they did not ask') indicts the leaders directly — they examined the physical evidence but skipped the theological check. The same verb root is embedded in the name 'Saul' (Šāʾûl, 'asked-for'), whose career is defined by the same failure.
15: wayyikrōt lāhem yĕhôšuaʿ šālôm wayyikrōt lāhem bĕrît — 'Joshua cut peace with them and cut a covenant.' Both verbs are kārat (cut), the standard covenant-making idiom ('cut a covenant'), heightened here by double use. Once sworn before YHWH (wayyiššābĕʿû lāhem nĕśîʾê hāʿēdāh), the oath is binding — the leaders of the congregation have staked their word before God. The rest of the narrative turns on whether an oath made under deception remains binding; the answer is consistently yes (Joshua 9:19-20).
16: miqṣēh šĕlōšet yāmîm — 'at the end of three days.' Three days is significant: not immediately (which would excuse a hasty treaty), not years later, but a short enough interval that the leaders cannot claim ignorance was unavoidable. The cities are named: Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, Kiriath-jearim — all in the territory of Benjamin, close to Jerusalem. The deceivers were neighbours.
17: The cities are Hivite (ḥiwwî), specifically listed, all within the central hill country. Their proximity to Jerusalem (Gibeon is ~9 km north) explains why their survival created long-term political tensions: these were not distant peoples but embedded enclaves in Israelite territory. The Benjaminite towns Beeroth and Kiriath-jearim figure in later narratives (the ark's resting places, 1 Samuel 7:1; 2 Samuel 6:2).
18: wayyillōnû kol-hāʿēdāh ʿal-hannĕśîʾîm — 'all the congregation grumbled against the leaders.' The verb lûn/lîn (grumble, murmur) is the characteristic word for Israel's wilderness rebellion (Exodus 15:24; Joshua 16:2; Numbers 14:2). The community's first instinct is violence — 'strike them down' — and the leaders' restraint preserves the oath's integrity over popular sentiment.
19: nišbaʿnû lāhem bYHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'we have sworn to them by YHWH the God of Israel.' The oath sworn in YHWH's name cannot be rescinded even though it was obtained by deception; violating it would be profaning YHWH's name. This becomes the foundation for Saul's later violation and the three-year famine (2 Samuel 21:1-2): YHWH holds Israel to an oath sworn in his name regardless of how it was obtained.
20: The formula wĕlōʾ yihyeh ʿālênû qeṣep — 'lest wrath come upon us' — frames oath-violation as a theological risk: YHWH's qeṣep (wrath, burning anger) would fall on Israel, not the Gibeonites, if Israel broke the oath. The leaders understand the covenant structure: once sworn before YHWH, the commitment runs toward God, not just toward the Gibeonites.
21: yihyû ḥōṭĕbê ʿēṣîm wĕšōʾăbê mayim lĕkol-hāʿēdāh — 'they will be woodcutters and water-carriers for all the congregation.' The permanent servile role preserves life within the covenant community. The phrase reappears at Joshua 9:23 and Joshua 9:27, where it is given a cultic dimension: service 'for the house of my God.' The Gibeonites are incorporated as a distinct service class — protected but not assimilated as equals.
22: Joshua's question is direct: lāmmāh rimmîtĕm ʾōtānû — 'why did you deceive us?' The verb rāmāh (deceive, betray) is strong, used of planned deception (Psalms 78:57; Lamentations 1:19). Joshua does not nullify the treaty but demands an accounting; the covenant stands, its terms redrawn.
23: wĕʿattāh ʾărûrîm ʾattem — 'and now cursed are you.' The ʾārûr formula is a formal covenant curse, not a personal imprecation. Yet the curse is immediately filled with specific content: perpetual servitude as woodcutters and water-carriers for 'the house of my God.' The curse is constructive — it assigns a permanent role within Israel's worship life. The Gibeonites are cursed into service, not cursed into destruction.
24: The Gibeonites' confession is remarkably candid: kî hoggēd hoggad lāʿăbādêkā ʾēt ʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā ʾet-mōšeh ʾabdô — 'because it was told to your servants what YHWH your God commanded his servant Moses.' They acted in self-preservation, knowing the ḥērem applied to them. Their fear was based on accurate theological knowledge: 'we were very afraid for our lives.'
25: wĕʿattāh hinnênû bĕyādekā kattôb wĕhayyāšār bĕʿênêkā laʿăśôt lānû ʿăśēh — 'now we are in your hand; do to us as it is good and right in your eyes.' Total submission. The phrase kattôb wĕhayyāšār ('good and right') is a covenant-loyalty formula, appealing to Joshua's integrity. They are throwing themselves on his character.
26: Joshua rescues them (wayyaṣṣēl ʾōtām) — the same verb used of divine rescue throughout the OT. The irony: the Gibeonites deceived their way into the covenant, yet the covenant's protection functions exactly as promised — Israel cannot harm them, and Joshua actively defends them. In Joshua 10, this protection obligation will require a costly all-night military march.
27: wayyittēn ʾōtām yĕhôšuaʿ bayyôm hahûʾ ḥōṭĕbê ʿēṣîm wĕšōʾăbê mayim lāʿēdāh wĕlĕmizbaḥ YHWH — assigned for 'the congregation and for the altar of YHWH, at whatever place he should choose.' The phrase ʾel-hamāqôm ʾašer yibḥar ('the place he will choose') is Deuteronomy's formula for the central sanctuary (Deuteronomy 12:5,11,14). The Gibeonites' future is tied to the future of Israelite worship — wherever the ark goes, they serve. This explains their presence at Nob (1 Samuel 22:19) and their later connection to Gibeon's high place (1 Kings 3:4).
